prompt: You spend most of your day writing code that users directly interact with.
Your work ships as something a non-technical person could open in a browser or app.
TypeScript is your default choice when starting a new project.
You have strong opinions about when to pick a framework and when to go vanilla.
You mostly build things that run directly inside a web browser.
You care deeply about how a component looks across different screen sizes.
You have written CSS that someone else later called beautiful.
Bundle size is something you actively track on every release.
You have filed an issue or PR against a component library you did not author.
Your daily work is more about visual components and design tokens than page loading speed.
You have submitted an app to the iOS or Android app stores at least once.
You think about platform differences between iOS and Android all the time.
You own the full request cycle from database query to rendered UI in a single feature.
You deploy your backend changes more often than you touch the native platform APIs.
You prefer shipping to an app store over deploying to a web server.
You have a personal opinion on the best way to structure a design token hierarchy.
Writing accessible markup feels as natural as writing functional code.
You use Storybook or a similar tool to develop components in isolation.
You have migrated a codebase from CSS modules to a CSS-in-JS solution or vice versa.
You think a well-structured component library is a competitive advantage for a product team.
You have profiled a page with Lighthouse and then actually fixed what it flagged.
Core Web Vitals show up in your sprint reviews as a real metric.
You have used WebAssembly to solve a problem that JavaScript was too slow for.
You have formed an opinion about partial hydration and server versus client rendering trade-offs.
You treat render blocking resources like tech debt that must be paid immediately.
You have strong feelings about React Native versus Flutter for a new project.
Push notification delivery rates are a number you monitor and care about.
You write platform-specific code when the cross-platform abstraction is not good enough.
App Store review guidelines feel like part of your product constraints.
You think deeply about offline mode and network conditions when designing mobile UX.
You have designed a REST or GraphQL API and later regretted some of those decisions.
You manage your own database schema migrations and own the data model end to end.
You enjoy shipping a complete feature without handing off work to another team.
Deployment pipelines and environment variables feel like first-class product concerns.
You have strong opinions about when to reach for a BaaS versus rolling your own server.
You prefer building with Tailwind CSS over writing custom component styles by hand.
You contribute to open-source component libraries in your spare time.
Motion and animation are part of how you think about component interactivity.
You frequently collaborate with product designers using Figma or similar tools.
You use WebGPU or WebGL to push rendering performance beyond what CSS can do.
You have built or seriously explored a PWA to replace a native app experience.
You have switched or considered switching bundlers specifically to improve cold start time.
You track real-user monitoring data to catch regressions before your team notices.
You reach for Jetpack Compose or SwiftUI rather than XML layouts or UIKit storyboards.
You have dealt with deep-link routing edge cases across iOS and Android.
App crash rate is a KPI you review regularly alongside user-facing metrics.
You have tuned battery or memory usage to pass app store performance reviews.
You prefer a monorepo where the frontend and backend share TypeScript types.
You have replaced a custom backend with Supabase or a similar managed solution and liked it.
You handle auth, payments, or third-party integrations as a solo contributor on your stack.
Picking the right data store for each workload is a decision you make early in any feature.
You enjoy building or maintaining the systems that other engineers depend on.
Deployments, pipelines, and uptime metrics are genuinely interesting topics to you.
You have written or reviewed configuration files more than application code this week.
Containers, clusters, and cloud providers feel like home territory to you.
You spend more time improving reliability and scale than hardening security or tooling.
You have personally written or applied Terraform or Pulumi code in production.
Multi-cloud strategy or cloud-provider cost optimisation is a regular concern for you.
You have strong opinions about how internal developer platforms should be designed.
Kubernetes is central to how your team ships and operates software today.
Your primary focus is provisioning and scaling cloud platforms rather than measuring reliability.
Your daily work touches threat modelling, vulnerability scanning, or secure code review.
You genuinely enjoy building tools that other developers use to do their jobs better.
You have worked on supply-chain security, secret scanning, or dependency auditing.
Build systems, compilers, or language runtimes attract you as much as shipped products do.
Securing systems from attack is more compelling to you than building developer tooling.
You manage multiple cloud environments and treat infrastructure drift as a first-class bug.
You have migrated a team from manual provisioning to a fully automated IaC workflow.
Kubernetes cluster upgrades and node pool management are familiar rituals for you.
You treat blast radius and rollback strategy as mandatory parts of any change proposal.
You actively follow cost-engineering and FinOps practices to keep cloud bills predictable.
You own or regularly contribute to your team's SLOs and error-budget policy.
You have instrumented services with OpenTelemetry or similar tracing frameworks.
You treat post-incident reviews as the most valuable engineering meeting on the calendar.
On-call rotations and runbook quality are engineering concerns you genuinely care about.
You read about distributed systems failure modes the way others read release notes.
You actively research CVEs, advisories, and zero-day disclosures as part of your workflow.
You have designed or reviewed authentication or authorisation systems for production services.
DevSecOps pipelines and automated security scanning are priorities you advocate for.
Threat modelling before a sprint starts feels like common sense rather than overhead to you.
You follow supply-chain attack trends and software bill-of-materials tooling closely.
You have built or shipped a CLI tool, VS Code extension, or developer-facing SDK.
You reach for Rust or Go when building performance-sensitive developer tooling.
You measure the success of your work in developer-hours saved across the organisation.
Build systems, monorepo tooling, or test-runner internals excite you as engineering problems.
You contribute to or closely follow open-source developer tools and language ecosystems.
You are comfortable writing custom Kubernetes controllers or operators when needed.
GitOps workflows like ArgoCD or Flux are part of how your team manages deployments.
You think about developer self-service and golden paths as much as raw infrastructure.
Runbooks, blast-radius limits, and infrastructure-as-code are your primary craft language.
You have set up or tuned Prometheus alerting rules for a production environment.
You invest time in chaos engineering or fault-injection to validate system resilience.
You have written capacity-planning documents or toil-reduction proposals for your team.
Reliability is a feature you design for from day one, not a concern you fix after launch.
You have conducted penetration tests or participated in bug-bounty programs.
Cryptography, key management, or secrets rotation are topics you follow with genuine interest.
You stay current on zero-day disclosures and consider proactive threat intel part of your job.
You believe shipping fast and sleeping at night are compatible when security is part of the design.
You care deeply about editor integration and language server protocol tooling.
You have strong opinions about package managers, lockfile formats, and dependency resolution.
Your pull requests frequently include improvements to CI speed, test ergonomics, or linting.
You measure your impact in keystrokes saved and friction removed for the engineers around you.
You regularly read about machine learning, data pipelines, or AI models.
Python is your go-to language for any data or modelling task.
You have real hands-on experience training or fine-tuning a model.
Storing and querying large datasets is a routine part of your work.
You are more interested in model behaviour than in pipeline reliability.
You follow the latest research papers on neural network architectures.
You have deployed a model to a production endpoint and monitored it.
GPU utilisation and CUDA kernel debugging feel familiar to you.
You prefer working close to the raw model weights rather than the API surface.
Experiment tracking and model evaluation pipelines are your daily tools.
You design and maintain data ingestion pipelines as your primary responsibility.
You hold strong opinions about streaming versus batch processing trade-offs.
SQL is the most powerful tool in your analytical workflow.
You care more about data freshness and reliability than dashboard aesthetics.
You spend more time orchestrating jobs than writing analytical queries.
You have a strong preference for PyTorch over TensorFlow for research projects.
Model serialisation and serving latency keep you up at night.
You write custom training loops rather than relying on high-level trainer APIs.
You track model drift and data distribution shift in production systems.
Reproducing a training run from a git hash and config file is non-negotiable for you.
You have shipped a user-facing feature that calls an LLM under the hood.
You spend time designing retrieval pipelines to ground LLM responses in real data.
Choosing between model providers is a recurring architectural decision for you.
You build orchestration layers that chain multiple AI calls together.
You think about AI latency and cost per token as product-level constraints.
You have built or maintained a multi-hop data pipeline across different systems.
Schema evolution and backwards compatibility in event streams concern you deeply.
You have tuned Spark jobs or Flink operators for throughput and cost.
Data lineage and observability tooling are part of your standard stack.
You model your data contracts before you write a single pipeline step.
Writing a clean dbt model feels as satisfying as shipping working code.
You maintain metric definitions that business stakeholders rely on every week.
You have built a self-serve analytics layer that reduced ad-hoc requests.
Funnel analysis and cohort retention are part of your regular reporting.
You prefer a well-defined semantic layer over raw table access for analysts.
Computer vision tasks get you more excited than NLP or text-based problems.
Reinforcement learning and reward modelling are on your reading list right now.
You have experimented with time-series forecasting for a real business problem.
Quantisation and model compression are techniques you consider before deploying.
You have integrated a vector database into an application you shipped.
You evaluate LLM outputs systematically with evals or automated scoring.
You have tuned system prompts across multiple iterations to improve accuracy.
You think deeply about agentic workflows and tool-calling patterns for LLMs.
You have migrated a data warehouse from on-prem to a cloud-native lakehouse.
Real-time streaming is your preferred approach over nightly batch loads.
You have debugged a data quality issue that silently corrupted downstream metrics.
Table format choices like Iceberg or Delta Lake matter for how you design storage.
You have designed a metrics store so teams share a single source of truth.
Statistical significance testing is part of how you validate product experiments.
You have replaced an opaque spreadsheet with a reproducible analytical model.
You advocate for data documentation as strongly as you advocate for test coverage.
Your day-to-day work lives firmly outside the mainstream web-and-cloud stack.
You regularly read about topics that most engineers would call niche.
You care more about outcomes for people than elegance of the code.
You think constraints — memory, budget, runtime — sharpen your best work.
Pixels, physics, and frame rates matter more to you than request latency.
You have written code that runs on silicon rather than a server rack.
A game engine is one of your primary development tools.
You have debugged hardware behaviour using an oscilloscope or logic analyser.
Frame pacing and draw call budgets are real concerns in your day-to-day work.
You care more about shipping a playable game than optimising kernel boot time.
You follow game-engine release notes the way others watch framework changelogs.
Writing custom shaders or render pipelines is something you genuinely enjoy.
You have shipped a game or interactive experience to real players.
Multiplayer networking and tick-rate tuning are familiar territory for you.
You benchmark your game build on target hardware before calling a feature done.
Most of your projects run on microcontrollers or bare-metal embedded hardware.
RTOS scheduling and interrupt latency are concerns you deal with hands-on.
You care about binary size and power draw as much as feature correctness.
Rust or C is your go-to when correctness and performance both matter.
Physical hardware in the loop is a normal part of your testing strategy.
You have managed or mentored a team of software engineers directly.
Equity cap tables and fundraising rounds occupy mental space in your week.
Growing the people around you matters as much as growing the product.
You are as comfortable pitching ideas to non-technical stakeholders as writing code.
Your primary job title right now is closer to founder or CEO than engineering manager.
You have shipped a product to paying customers you found yourself.
Investor updates and fundraising decks are part of your regular writing output.
You still write production code even though you own the company vision.
Hiring your first or next key engineering hire is on your mind right now.
You read content about building defensible distribution as much as building product.
You own a team's delivery commitments and protect their focus from distraction.
One-on-ones and performance conversations are a scheduled part of your week.
You measure your impact by what your team ships when you are not present.
You actively shape hiring bar, team culture, and engineering values at your company.
Technical strategy — roadmaps, architecture decisions, build vs buy — lands on your desk.
You work primarily with a major commercial engine like Unity or Unreal.
Indie solo or small-team game dev is your primary creative outlet.
Low-level graphics APIs like Vulkan, Metal, or WebGPU are on your reading list.
Nailing game feel — juicy feedback, responsive controls — is a point of professional pride.
You write firmware that talks directly to sensors or actuators over a serial bus.
IoT connectivity and edge compute are closer to your work than pure bare-metal code.
Robotics, motor control, or physical automation is part of what you build.
You prefer Rust over C for new embedded projects when the toolchain supports it.
Distribution and go-to-market keep you up at night as much as the tech stack does.
You have taken VC money or are actively exploring institutional funding.
Open-source traction is a deliberate part of your company-building strategy.
You still enjoy reading a sharp engineering post even when you are running the company.
You have made the call to rewrite, retire, or hard-pivot a core system in production.
People-management — career paths, promotion calibration — takes more of your time than code.
You champion engineering culture and psychological safety as deliberate team investments.
Your team's output when you step away is the clearest measure of your leadership.
